context.problem: Governance artifacts can evolve without a durable, queryable record of why a change was made, what alternatives were rejected, and what CI obligations the decision creates.
forces: ADR prose is readable but weakly enforceable.
Schema-only governance records shape but not rationale.
Architecture decisions must remain public-safe and abstracted.

options_considered.assessment: Easy to read but difficult to validate, query, or connect into the governance graph.
Machine-readable and CI-friendly, but less readable without generated documentation.

options_considered.option: Dual representation from one canonical JSON source
options_considered.assessment: Best long-term path: validate JSON as source of truth, then generate readable notes later.

decision.summary: Adopt governance.adr.v1 as the canonical machine-readable decision artifact for governance changes.
requirements: Every intentional governance change must reference an ADR or open ADR proposal.
Accepted ADRs must be indexable as governance graph nodes.
ADR lifecycle status must be explicit and schema-valid.
Private or personal material must be abstracted into public-safe architectural concerns.

positive: Decision rationale becomes queryable and durable.
CI can verify that governance changes have decision backing.
Supersession can be represented as graph edges.
negative: Decision writing requires more structure up front.
Readable Markdown views will need generation or manual summaries.
follow_up: Define failure fixtures for rejected lifecycle and missing public-safety fields.

public_safety.redaction_rule: Record architecture-level concerns only; omit names, health details, locations, private conversations, and raw emotional material.

compatibility.migration_note: Initial ADR contract; no previous ADR schema exists.
